content: I'm looking to introduce a common Rust target for kernel/firmware/standalone usage. I'm interested in making that target as close to usable for the Linux kernel as possible. To what extent does the current work depend on the x86_64-unknown-none-linuxkernel target, and to what extent might it be possible to use a more general x86_64-unknown-none target if one existed?

I'm trying to find out, in particular, whether there's value in having a separate kernel-specific target (in which case I may still be able to factor out common elements but keep the kernel-specific target built atop those) or whether a non-kernel-specific standalone target might suffice. Either way is fine, I'd just like to coordinate.
I believe we stopped using the linuxkernel target at some point and now have our own target in-tree.
@Alex Gaynor Interesting. Would it be possible to make an upstream target useful enough to help ameliorate that?
(I know that you can't use upstream builds of core, but I'm wondering if upstream target support could be useful.)
If not, should the x86_64-unknown-none-linuxkernel target be dropped upstream?
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/28 is the PR where we did it...which was like 900 years ago apparently :-)
Makes sense. I wonder, then, if we should expose more of the options you need as codegen options rather than target-spec-only options, and then give a baseline target that's mostly what you want.
